evals.prompt: I want to build a task management app with Next.js, Supabase, and Tailwind. Users should be able to create projects, add tasks, assign them, and track progress. Can you help me plan this out?
evals.expected_output: A complete SPEC document following the template with all 5 sections: Overview, Pages, Components, Behaviors, and Data Model. The spec should cover the task management domain thoroughly.
expectations: The output includes a SPEC document with all required sections
The spec defines the technical stack (Next.js, Supabase, Tailwind)
The spec includes at least 3 pages (e.g., dashboard, project detail, tasks)
The spec includes a data model with tables for projects, tasks, and users
The spec defines RLS policies for the tables
The spec includes user flows for core behaviors
The spec covers error scenarios, not just happy paths

evals.prompt: I have this spec for a blog platform. Break it into issues: The blog has posts with title, content, slug, and published_at. Users can register, login, create posts, edit their own posts, and admins can delete any post. Stack is Next.js + Prisma + PostgreSQL.
evals.expected_output: A set of issues organized by layers (Foundation, Visual, Features, Polish) with clear scope, file paths, dependencies, and acceptance criteria.
expectations: Issues follow the template format with Context, Objective, Scope DO/DON'T, Files, Acceptance Criteria, Dependencies
Issues are organized by layers: Foundation first, then Visual, Features, Polish
Each issue modifies at most 5-7 files
Issues include negative scope (what NOT to do)
Issues list exact file paths
Dependencies between issues are clearly defined
Database schema issue comes before feature issues

evals.prompt: I need to execute ISSUE-3 which is about implementing user authentication with email/password using Supabase Auth. The stack is Next.js 14 with App Router. Create the server actions, auth hooks, and login/register components.
evals.expected_output: Implementation following the agent execution order: model (if needed), action (server actions for auth), component (login/register forms), hook (useAuth). Each piece follows the framework rules.
expectations: Server actions follow the { success, data, error } return pattern
Input validation is present (Zod or similar)
Components handle loading, empty, error, success states
API keys are not exposed in frontend code
Business logic stays on the server
Auth checks are implemented in server actions

evals.prompt: I'm starting a new e-commerce project. Give me the execution plan for the 'Setup project and database schema' issue. The project uses Next.js 14, Stripe for payments, Supabase for database and auth.
evals.expected_output: A detailed execution plan with internal research, external research, technical decisions, file-by-file implementation detail, and test scenarios.
expectations: Plan includes internal research section checking for existing code
Plan includes external research for Next.js and Supabase documentation
Plan details technical decisions with alternatives considered
Each file to be created/modified is listed with action, description, exports, imports
Test scenarios are defined for the setup issue

evals.prompt: Help me understand when to use each of the 7 specialized agents. I'm building a social media dashboard with React and I'm confused about the execution order.
evals.expected_output: Clear explanation of each agent's role (model-writer through test-writer), when to use each, and why the order matters. Should reference the framework's agent system.
expectations: All 7 agents are explained: model-writer, action-writer, route-writer, component-writer, hook-writer, integration-writer, test-writer
The execution order is explained: model → action → route → component → hook → integration → test
The reasoning behind the order is clear (dependencies flow in that direction)
Examples are given relevant to a social media dashboard
